content:
6 min read 10 hours ago -- A clear, practical guide to how Angular detects changes today — and why it changed. Press enter or click to view image in full size Angular Signals have quietly changed how the framework keeps your UI in sync with your data. If you have written Angular for a while, you have probably never had to think much about how the screen updates when your data changes. It just happened. That convenience came from a library called Zone.js, and understanding what it was doing — and why Angular is moving away from it — is one of the more rewarding things you can learn about the framework right now. This post walks through the whole story, step by step: how change detection used to work, how Signals change it, and where all of this stands in Angular today. No prior knowledge of Signals is assumed. The way it used to work: Zone.js For most of Angular’s life, change detection was powered by Zone.js. Zone.js works by patching the browser’s asynchronous APIs — things like setTimeout, addEventListener, Promise, and network requests. Whenever one of those finishes, Zone.js notifies Angular that something might have changed. The important word there is “might.” Zone.js never actually knew whether your data had changed. It only knew that an asynchronous operation had completed. So to stay safe, Angular would respond by running change detection and walking through the component tree, checking each binding to make sure the UI matched the latest state. It is worth clearing up a common misconception here: Angular never blindly re-renders your components. It checks your bindings, and only updates the specific DOM nodes that actually differ. So the waste was never in updating the screen. The waste was in all the checking — Angular had to look broadly, because Zone.js could not tell it where the change actually happened. This is the trade-off the classic model made: • Automatic detection. Angular watched the entire application for events that might change data. • Broad updates. When something fired, Angular often checked the whole component tree to be safe. • The cost. It required shipping the extra Zone.js library, which increased bundle size, and it tended to do more work than was strictly necessary. It worked well, and for years it was good enough. But “check everything, just in case” is the kind of design that becomes worth improving once a framework matures. How Signals change the picture Signals take a more precise approach. Instead of Angular watching the whole app and guessing, you tell Angular exactly when your data changes and exactly where it is used. A signal is simply a wrapper around a value that can notify Angular when that value changes. The key idea is this: when you read a signal in your template, Angular remembers that exact location. So when the value updates later, Angular already knows precisely which parts of the UI depend on it, and it refreshes only those — instead of re-checking everything. That gives you three concrete benefits: • Granular updates. Angular tracks where each signal is read. When the value changes, it updates only the specific parts of the UI that use it, rather than re-checking entire components. • Smaller bundle size. As applications move fully to Signals, the need for Zone.js disappears, which leads to smaller, faster-loading apps. • Predictable control. You gain precise, explicit control over how and when your state changes propagate to the screen. The core API: three things to learn The entire foundation of Signals comes down to three operations. Create a signal with the signal() function. It can hold any data type — a number, a string, an object, anything. Update a signal with one of two methods. Use set() to replace the value entirely, or update() to derive a new value from the previous one. Read a signal by calling it as a function, for example mySignal(). Calling it is what lets Angular register the signal as a dependency in that specific location. That is genuinely the whole core. Everything else builds on these three ideas. A practical example: a user profile manager Let’s make this concrete with a small component that manages a user’s profile. It holds a username and an online status, and lets you change both. Here is the component itself: user-profile.component.ts import { Component, signal } from '@angular/core';@Component({ selector: 'app-user-profile', standalone: true, templateUrl: './user-profile.component.html', }) export class UserProfileComponent { // 1. Initialize a signal with a value. // This creates a reactive wrapper around the data. username = signal('Guest_User'); isOnline = signal(false); updateUsername(newName: string) { // 2. Use set() to overwrite the current value directly. this.username.set(newName); } toggleStatus() { // 3. Use update() to derive the new value from the old one. this.isOnline.update(prevStatus => !prevStatus); } }And here is the template that uses it: user-profile.component.html

User: {{ username() }}

Status: {{ isOnline() ? 'Online' : 'Offline' }}

Here is the part worth pausing on. Because you wrote username() and isOnline() in the template, Angular has memorised exactly where each signal is read. So when you click the button and toggleStatus() runs, Angular updates only the

tag bound to the status. The

showing the username is never touched. That is the granularity Zone.js could never offer. Zone.js operated at the level of “an event happened somewhere, so check things.” Signals operate at the level of “this specific value, read in this specific place, changed.” Going further: dropping Zone.js entirely Granular updates solve how much Angular checks. There is a second, related improvement that solves when Angular checks: running without Zone.js at all, often called “zoneless” mode. In a zoneless app, change detection no longer fires on every patched async call. Instead, it runs only when Angular receives an explicit notification — for example, when a signal read in a template gets a new value, when a template event fires, or when an async pipe emits. The result is that change detection runs only where it is needed and exactly when it is needed. Removing Zone.js also has a direct, measurable payoff: your bundle shrinks by roughly 33KB. That is not enormous on its own, but because Zone.js has to be downloaded and parsed before your app even starts, dropping it translates straight into a faster initial load. A few things to watch out for Moving from implicit reactivity to explicit reactivity means you have to be a little more deliberate. Two patterns commonly trip people up. First, reading a signal once does not make your code reactive. This, for example, reads the value a single time and never tracks future changes: // This reads once. It does NOT react to later changes. ngOnInit() { const value = this.count(); }If you want to run a side effect whenever a signal changes, use effect(): // This re-runs automatically whenever count changes. effect(() => { console.log('Count changed:', this.count()); });Second, mutating a value in place will not trigger an update, because the signal still holds the same reference and sees nothing new: const items = signal([1, 2, 3]); items().push(4); // Nothing updates — same array reference.Instead, produce a new value so the signal has something to detect: items.update(current => [...current, 4]); // This updates correctly.Neither of these is difficult. They are just the natural consequence of Angular handing you explicit control: it now does exactly what you tell it, no more and no less. Where this stands in Angular today If you have read about Signals before and filed them away as a “developer preview” or a future direction, it is time to update that mental model. This is no longer experimental, and it is no longer optional for new projects. • Signals were introduced as a developer preview in Angular 16. • They became stable in Angular 20 (May 2025). • Zoneless change detection became the default for new projects in Angular 21 (November 2025). • Angular 22, released in June 2026, makes the signal-first model the production default, with zoneless architecture, OnPush change detection, and Signal Forms all stable. In other words, the future people were anticipating a year ago is simply how new Angular applications are built now. The bigger idea Strip away the version numbers and the API, and there is one shift underneath all of this: Angular moved from implicit reactivity, where the framework guesses on your behalf, to explicit reactivity, where you declare exactly what depends on what. You give up a little of the “it just works” magic. In return, you get smaller bundles, better runtime performance, and updates that are far easier to reason about. For anyone who has ever spent an afternoon chasing down a mysterious extra change detection cycle, that is a trade well worth making.
